planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is warm; Crown Prince requires a long warm season (95–110 days) and ample space for the vigorous sprawling vines (up to 12 feet). Harvest when the skin has hardened and turned from green to its characteristic steel-blue-grey tone; the skin does not 'slip' like butternut but the stem begins to dry. Store in a cool, dry location for outstanding long-term keeping (up to 12 months).

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills (3–4 seeds per hill, thin to 2 plants) after last frost in rich, well-drained soil; harvest when the stem begins to cork and dry and the skin is completely firm. Crown Prince is a Cucurbita maxima winter squash of Japanese origin that became a dominant market squash in the United Kingdom and Australia and has grown in popularity in the US — producing medium-large (6–12 lb) flat-lobed fruits with an unusual and striking steel-blue to silver-grey skin color that makes them one of the most distinctive vegetables in the fall garden. The skin color is unlike any other commonly grown squash and makes Crown Prince exceptional as both a culinary and ornamental squash. The flesh is deep orange, dense, smooth-textured, and richly sweet with a complex, almost butterscotch-like flavor profile that many tasters consider among the best of any squash; it lacks the wateriness of less concentrated types and has a low seed cavity relative to flesh volume. Crown Prince keeps exceptionally well (6–12 months in proper cool, dry storage) and the long storage window makes it valuable for extended autumn and winter cooking. In zone 9, direct sow in March–April for a July–August harvest, or in August for a November harvest if the growing season allows. An excellent choice for serious squash growers wanting a variety with both superior flavor and visual impact.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Squash Crown Prince variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Winter Squash Production Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Production

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or rooted tissue-culture transplants in late winter or early spring; Apache is a biennial-caning plant (floricanes fruit in year two) so first full crop is the second season after planting. Space rows 6–8 feet apart for mechanized harvesting or 4–6 feet for home gardens. Chill hours required: approximately 300–500 hours below 45°F — suitable for zone 7–8 Gulf Coast winter conditions.

directions: Plant rooted canes or tissue-culture transplants in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil; prune floricanes to the ground after harvest to maintain plant vigor. Apache is a 2003 release from the University of Arkansas blackberry breeding program (the same program that produced Arapaho, Navaho, Kiowa, and Ouachita) and produces some of the largest blackberry fruits in commercial production — individual berries averaging 14–17 grams (roughly 3–4 times the size of a typical wild blackberry), with an excellent balance of sweetness and mild acidity that makes them outstanding for fresh eating. The thornless, erect canes (4–6 feet tall) require no support structures and are easy and pleasant to harvest without the scratches common to thorny types. Apache ripens in late July in Arkansas (later than Arapaho and Navaho), extending the fresh blackberry season into peak summer. The large, firm berries hold their quality well after harvest, giving Apache better shipping and shelf life than softer varieties — an advantage for market growers and home gardeners who want berries for preserving. Very productive when properly established, and the thornless, self-supporting habit makes it the most garden-friendly of the Arkansas blackberry releases.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Apache blackberry variety release
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blackberry Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Small Fruit Variety Recommendations

common_name: Pineapple Pear (Gulf South Low-Chill Heirloom Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) or container-grown trees in fall or winter; Pineapple pear requires only approximately 150–250 chill hours (hours below 45°F), making it one of the most heat-adapted pear varieties for the Gulf Coast. A pollenizer (Orient, Flordahome, or Garber) improves fruit set. Trees bear first crop in 3–5 years from planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pineapple pear is one of the most widely grown and historically important heirloom pear varieties in the Gulf South, deeply rooted in the orchard traditions of Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and Florida — a Pyrus communis selection known for its unusually low chill hour requirement (approximately 150–250 hours) that makes it productive in Gulf Coast winters that defeat most European pear varieties. The medium-large, yellowish-green to golden-tan fruits have a distinctly sweet, rich, aromatic quality with a subtle pineapple-like fragrance and flavor that distinguishes them from blander pear types; the flesh is moderately juicy and slightly gritty (typical of P. communis), excellent for fresh eating and very good for canning and preserves. Pineapple pear shows useful fire blight resistance — critical in the humid Gulf South where fire blight (Erwinia amylovora) devastates susceptible varieties — and the trees are productive and long-lived in appropriate climates. Ripens in late July through August in the Gulf South. This variety has sustained Southern home orchards and farmstead preserving traditions for generations and remains one of the recommended low-chill pear varieties for zone 8–10 Gulf Coast production.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pear Variety Recommendations for Louisiana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pear Production in Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Fruit and Nut Varieties for Texas

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in late winter or spring in rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, deep soil in full sun to partial shade; Shenandoah, like all pawpaws, requires a different-variety pollenizer for fruit set (plant at least 2 different named cultivars). Trees are typically produced as grafted stock for true-to-type production and begin bearing fruit 3–7 years after planting; 5 years to meaningful production is typical.

directions: Plant grafted trees in rich, well-drained, deep, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in full sun to partial shade; young trees (first 2 years) prefer shade protection and benefit from a shade cloth until established. Shenandoah is one of the most acclaimed modern pawpaw cultivars from the Neal Peterson Pawpaw breeding program — a selection made from Peterson's 'Overleese' strain after decades of rigorous selection for fruit size, flavor, and consistency. The large fruits (5–11 oz, averaging 6–8 oz) have fewer seeds than typical wild pawpaws, a higher flesh-to-seed ratio, and are considered one of the mildest and sweetest of the named pawpaw cultivars with a smooth, vanilla-custard flavor without the more tropical, overpowering sweetness of some other cultivars — making Shenandoah more accessible to eaters unfamiliar with pawpaws and a frequently recommended starting variety. The creamy yellow flesh is rich and buttery with an extraordinarily smooth texture unlike any other common temperate fruit. Pawpaw trees require hand-pollination or fly-and-beetle pollination (not bee-pollinated) for reliable fruit set, and planting two different-variety cultivars for cross-pollination is essential. Shenandoah ripens in early to mid-September in Virginia; slightly later in zone 9 conditions. Peterson Pawpaws holds plant patents on several named cultivars including Shenandoah.
sources: Peterson Pawpaws — Shenandoah variety notes and breeding history
Kentucky State University Cooperative Extension — Pawpaw Cultivar Evaluation
Ohio State University Extension — Growing Pawpaws

common_name: Painted Pony Bean (Speckled Heirloom Dry Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; Painted Pony is a determinate bush dry bean that sets pods over a 2–3 week window. Allow pods to dry on the vine until the pods are tan, papery, and the beans rattle inside, then harvest and finish drying indoors before shelling. In zone 9, a short spring sowing (March–April) before summer heat sets in is ideal.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 60°F; allow pods to fully mature and dry on the vine before harvesting. Painted Pony is a visually spectacular heirloom dry bean producing medium-sized beans with a beautiful swirled pattern of deep maroon-red, tan, and cream — each bean unique in its markings, resembling the coat of an Appaloosa or pinto horse, which gives the variety its evocative name. The ornamental markings unfortunately fade significantly when cooked (as with most colorfully marked dry beans), but the flavor is excellent — rich, slightly earthy, and starchy with the substantial body that makes it outstanding in soups, chilis, stews, and baked bean preparations. Painted Pony beans hold their shape reasonably well when cooked, making them suitable for preparations where bean integrity matters. A productive bush-type bean (no staking required) that fits well into small gardens and is an outstanding choice for seed saving due to its distinctive and reliably reproduced markings. An excellent heirloom variety for bean enthusiasts who value genetic and visual diversity in their garden and pantry.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Painted Pony bean variety notes
Native Seeds/SEARCH — Heirloom bean germplasm collection
